summary: Gruber dissects a CNBC report on Tim Cook's Good Morning America interview, calling the headline claiming Cook 'squashed' retirement rumors journalistic malpractice. Cook's actual words were a masterfully crafted non-answer that would remain technically accurate whether he steps down next month or stays for years. Gruber then systematically tears apart CNBC's framing of recent Apple executive departures as 'turbulent,' correcting the record on Giannandrea (effectively fired months earlier), Adams and Jackson (normal retirements), Dye (his departure is widely seen as good news for Apple), and Srouji (the Bloomberg story was likely bogus and possibly planted by Meta). The piece concludes that CNBC's credulous reporting may have served Meta's interests by seeding doubt about Apple's leadership.
key_points: Cook's GMA answer about retirement was a carefully constructed non-denial — true whether he leaves in two weeks or stays five more years — not a 'squash' of anything.

Giannandrea wasn't part of a turbulent departure wave; he was effectively fired in April/May 2025 when Cook stripped his responsibilities, with the December 'retirement' announcement just a face-saving formality.
Alan Dye's departure to Meta is viewed positively inside and outside Apple — Gruber argues Dye was a fraud who damaged macOS and never belonged at Apple.
The Srouji-threatening-Cook story requires believing a cascade of implausible things simultaneously, and Srouji's own memo effectively retracted it.
Gruber floats the theory that Meta deliberately planted the Srouji story to extend the positive narrative from poaching Dye, and CNBC fell for it.
money_quotes: It's an incredibly deft non-answer that would remain true if Cook steps down as CEO in two weeks, on April 1 (Apple's anniversary), and would remain true if he's still CEO five years from now.
This headline is journalistic malpractice from CNBC.
Not just that Dye is a fraud of a UI designer. Not just that he and his inner circle have vandalized MacOS, the crown jewel of human-computer interaction.
That he left for Meta, of all fucking companies? That's the proof that Dye (and his urban cowboy magazine-designer cohort) never belonged at Apple in the first place.
It's also possible that monkeys might fly out of my butt.
Mission accomplished, at least with the gullible reporters and editors at CNBC.
Cook answered by saying he's not political and only cares about policy, which makes sense only if you believe government policy decisions aren't political — which is to say it makes no sense.
key_insight: Cook's retirement non-answer was a masterclass in deniability, and CNBC's failure to recognize it — combined with credulous reporting on executive departures — may have made them unwitting amplifiers of Meta's PR strategy.
